text.title: Backend Languages Practices Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: package userservice defines type UserRepository interface alongside the Postgres implementation.
antipattern.root-cause: Author copied a Java/C# pattern of declaring interfaces with the implementation.
antipattern.fix: Move the interface to the consumer package. Implementation package returns the concrete struct; consumers depend on their own narrow interface.

antipattern.symptom: Tutorial-style code: @Autowired private UserRepository repo; in service classes.
antipattern.root-cause: Field injection is shown in most tutorials; agent replicated the public pattern.
antipattern.fix: Convert to constructor injection: private final UserRepository repo + @RequiredArgsConstructor. Re-run unit tests without Spring context.

antipattern.symptom: reqwest::blocking::Client::new().get(url) appears inside an async fn under #[tokio::main].
antipattern.root-cause: Code compiles; agent did not realise blocking calls freeze the async runtime.
antipattern.fix: Switch to reqwest::Client (async) and .await the response. For unavoidable blocking work, use tokio::task::spawn_blocking.

antipattern.symptom: Controller action contains 50+ lines of business logic, DB writes, mail sends.
antipattern.root-cause: Author followed the path of least resistance; no service object pattern in place.
antipattern.fix: Extract logic to a service object under app/services/; controller calls service.call and renders the result.
